title: Split interrupt handling top and bottom half
description: Do minimal acknowledgement in the hard IRQ handler and defer real work to a softirq/tasklet/threaded handler so interrupts stay responsive.
body: Use when writing a device driver's interrupt path. Do not do long processing or take sleeping locks in the hard IRQ handler, and do not leave interrupts disabled longer than necessary. Actions: in the top half acknowledge the device and schedule a bottom half (threaded IRQ, tasklet, or workqueue) for the heavy work; pick a threaded handler when the work may sleep. Validation signals include low measured IRQ-disabled time and no missed interrupts under load. Risk boundary: a slow hard IRQ handler raises latency for the whole system and can drop interrupts; treat unbounded work in the top half as a defect and verify latency under interrupt load.
